book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
title: Proposed Action on Clerical Celibacy.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Often the mind of a deliberative assembly is as clearly shown by the propositions it rejects as by those it adopts, and it would seem that this doctrine is of application in the case of the asserted attempt at this Council to pass a decree forbidding the priesthood to live in the use of marriage.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: This attempt is said to have failed.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The particulars are as follows:

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Councils, Vol. I., pp.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 435 et seqq.)

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Socrates, Sozomen, and Gelasius affirm that the Synod of Nicæa, as well as that of Elvira (can.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 33), desired to pass a law respecting celibacy.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: This law was to forbid all bishops, priests and deacons (Sozomen adds subdeacons), who were married at the time of their ordination, to continue to live with their wives.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: But, say these historians, the law was opposed openly and decidedly by Paphnutius, bishop of a city of the Upper Thebaïs in Egypt, a man of a high reputation, who had lost an eye during the persecution under Maximian.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: He was also celebrated for his miracles, and was held in so great respect by the Emperor, that the latter often kissed the empty socket of the lost eye.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Paphnutius declared with a loud voice, “that too heavy a yoke ought not to be laid upon the clergy; that marriage and married intercourse are of themselves honourable and undefiled; that the Church ought not to be injured by an extreme severity, for all could not live in absolute continency: in this way (by not prohibiting married intercourse) the virtue of the wife would be much more certainly preserved (viz. the wife of a clergyman, because she might find injury elsewhere, if her husband withdrew from her married intercourse).

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The intercourse of a man with his lawful wife may also be a chaste intercourse.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: It would therefore be sufficient, according to the ancient tradition of the Church, if those who had taken holy orders without being married were prohibited from marrying afterwards; but those clergymen who had been married only once as laymen, were not to be separated from their wives (Gelasius adds, or being only a reader or cantor).”

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: This discourse of Paphnutius made so much the more impression, because he had never lived in matrimony himself, and had had no conjugal intercourse.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Paphnutius, indeed, had been brought up in a monastery, and his great purity of manners had rendered him especially celebrated.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Therefore the Council took the serious words of the Egyptian bishop into consideration, stopped all discussion upon the law, and left to each cleric the responsibility of deciding the point as he would.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: If this account be true, we must conclude that a law was proposed to the Council of Nicæa the same as one which had been carried twenty years previously at Elvira, in Spain; this coincidence would lead us to believe that it was the Spaniard Hosius who proposed the law respecting celibacy at Nicæa.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The discourse ascribed to Paphnutius, and the consequent decision of the Synod, agree very well with the text of the Apostolic Constitutions, and with the whole practice of the Greek Church in respect to celibacy.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The Greek Church as well as the Latin accepted the principle, that whoever had taken holy orders before marriage, ought not to be married afterwards.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: In the Latin Church, bishops, priests, deacons. and even subdeacons, were considered to be subject to this law, because the latter were at a very early period reckoned among the higher servants of the Church, which was not the case in the Greek Church.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The Greek Church went so far as to allow deacons to marry after their ordination, if previously to it they had expressly obtained from their bishop permission to do so.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The Council of Ancyra affirms this (c.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: We see that the Greek Church wishes to leave the bishop free to decide the matter; but in reference to priests, it also prohibited them from marrying after their ordination.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Therefore, whilst the Latin Church exacted of those presenting themselves for ordination, even as subdeacons, that they should not continue to live with their wives if they were married, the Greek Church gave no such prohibition; but if the wife of an ordained clergyman died, the Greek Church allowed no second marriage.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The Apostolic Constitutions decided this point in the same way.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: To leave their wives from a pretext of piety was also forbidden to Greek priests; and the Synod of Gangra (c.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 4) took up the defence of married priests against the Eustathians.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Eustathius, however, was not alone among the Greeks in opposing the marriage of all clerics, and in desiring to introduce into the Greek Church the Latin discipline on this point.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: St. Epiphanius also inclined towards this side.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The Greek Church did not, however, adopt this rigour in reference to priests, deacons, and subdeacons, but by degrees it came to be required of bishops and of the higher order of clergy in general, that they should live in celibacy.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Yet this was not until after the compilation of the Apostolic Canons (c.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 5) and of the Constitutions; for in those documents mention is made of bishops living in wedlock, and Church history shows that there were married bishops, for instance Synesius, in the fifth century.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: But it is fair to remark, even as to Synesius, that he made it an express condition of his acceptation, on his election to the episcopate, that he might continue to live the married life.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Thomassin believes that Synesius did not seriously require this condition, and only spoke thus for the sake of escaping the episcopal office; which would seem to imply that in his time Greek bishops had already begun to live in celibacy.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: At the Trullan Synod (c.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 13.) the Greek Church finally settled the question of the marriage of priests.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Baronius, Valesius, and other historians, have considered the account of the part taken by Paphnutius to be apocryphal.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Baronius says, that as the Council of Nicæa in its third canon gave a law upon celibacy it is quite impossible to admit that it would alter such a law on account of Paphnutius.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: But Baronius is mistaken in seeing a law upon celibacy in that third canon; he thought it to be so, because, when mentioning the women who might live in the clergyman’s house—his mother, sister, etc.—the canon does not say a word about the wife.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: It had no occasion to mention her, it was referring to the συνεισάκτοι whilst these συνεισάκτοι and married women have nothing in common.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Natalis Alexander gives this anecdote about Paphnutius in full: he desired to refute Ballarmin, who considered it to be untrue and an invention of Socrates to please the Novatians.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Natalis Alexander often maintains erroneous opinions, and on the present question he deserves no confidence.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: If, as St. Epiphanius relates, the Novatians maintained that the clergy might be married exactly like the laity, it cannot be said that Socrates shared that opinion, since he says, or rather makes Paphnutius say, that, according to ancient tradition, those not married at the time of ordination should not be so subsequently.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Moreover, if it may be said that Socrates had a partial sympathy with the Novatians, he certainly cannot be considered as belonging to them, still less can he be accused of falsifying history in their favour.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: He may sometimes have propounded erroneous opinions, but there is a great difference between that and the invention of a whole story.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Valesius especially makes use of the argument ex silentio against Socrates. (a) Rufinus, he says, gives many particulars about Paphnutius in his History of the Church; he mentions his martyrdom, his miracles, and the Emperor’s reverence for him, but not a single word of the business about celibacy. (b) The name of Paphnutius is wanting in the list of Egyptian bishops present at the Synod.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: These two arguments of Valesius are weak; the second has the authority of Rufinus himself against it, who expressly says that Bishop Paphnutius was present at the Council of Nicæa.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: If Valesius means by lists only the signatures at the end of the acts of the Council, this proves nothing; for these lists are very imperfect, and it is well known that many bishops whose names are not among these signatures were present at Nicæa.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: This argument ex silentio is evidently insufficient to prove that the anecdote about Paphnutius must be rejected as false, seeing that it is in perfect harmony with the practice of the ancient Church, and especially of the Greek Church, on the subject of clerical marriages.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: On the other hand, Thomassin pretends that there was no such practice, and endeavours to prove by quotations from St. Epiphanius, St. Jerome, Eusebius, and St. John Chrysostom, that even in the East priests who were married at the time of their ordination were prohibited from continuing to live with their wives.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The texts quoted by Thomassin prove only that the Greeks gave especial honour to priests living in perfect continency, but they do not prove that this continence was a duty incumbent upon all priests; and so much the less, as the fifth and twenty-fifth Apostolic canons, the fourth canon of Gangra, and the thirteenth of the Trullan Synod, demonstrate clearly enough what was the universal custom of the Greek Church on this point.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Lupus and Phillips explained the words of Paphnutius in another sense.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: According to them, the Egyptian bishop was not speaking in a general way; he simply desired that the contemplated law should not include the subdeacons.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: But this explanation does not agree with the extracts quoted from Socrates, Sozomen, and Gelasius, who believe Paphnutius intended deacons and priests as well.
